answer:
Through this field experience and visual narrative project, I learned that love at UCSB often appears through ordinary social interactions rather than only through romantic relationships. The observations from the Japanese Student Association fundraiser and the intramural soccer community consistently reflected themes of friendship, mentorship, and cultural belonging that come from passion. In many of the moments I observed, students created meaningful connections simply by spending time together, encouraging one another, and sharing common interests or experiences. These interactions demonstrated that love can function as a form of emotional security that helps students feel less isolated within a large social environment.
The Unit 2 readings are strongly connected to my observations because the authors consistently explained how human connections reduce fear, stress, and loneliness. Arthur C. Brooks argues that supportive communities help people feel emotionally secure, while Diane Ackerman explains that shared experiences strengthen emotional bonds and reduce stress. Similarly, Mandy Len Catron suggests that emotional closeness develops through meaningful interaction and openness. My field observations confirmed these ideas because the communities I observed created welcoming environments where students appeared comfortable, connected, and emotionally supported.
Overall, this project helped me understand that love at UCSB is often expressed through actions rather than words. Through teamwork, mentorship, cultural connection, and friendship, students create communities that strengthen belonging and emotional well-being across campus.
